title: TreeSize won't renew perpetual-license support unless users subscribe
gist: JAM Software, maker of the disk-analyzer TreeSize, has moved most editions to subscriptions and now refuses to provide license keys or installers to perpetual-license holders once their 12-month support period lapses, citing security risk and economic pressure as justification.

themes.theme: Subscription conversion of a legacy perpetual product

insights.claim: Since 2025, JAM Software has transitioned most TreeSize editions to subscription models, now selling perpetual licenses only for personal use, which include 12 months of updates, support, and access to older-version downloads, with an option to extend the support period.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A concrete timeline and mechanism for how a legacy perpetual-license software vendor pivots toward recurring revenue.

insights.claim: Once a perpetual license's maintenance period expires, customers must subscribe to continue receiving updates and support, since JAM Software states those are now only available through the subscription.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Illustrates a common pattern where 'perpetual' licenses are perpetual only for continued use of the version bought, not for support or upgrades.
insights.quote: are only available in the subscription.

insights.claim: JAM Software product manager Hendrik Christ says there are no plans to discontinue selling perpetual licenses for TreeSize Personal specifically, even as most other editions move to subscription.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Shows the vendor is preserving a perpetual option for a lower-value consumer segment while monetizing business users via subscription.
insights.quote: no plans to discontinue the sale of perpetual licenses for TreeSize Personal

themes.theme: Withholding of installers and license keys

insights.claim: JAM Software will not provide backup installation files or license keys to customers whose perpetual-license support has lapsed, and this policy predates the subscription shift, according to Christ.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A key customer risk: buyers must proactively back up their own installer and key or lose access entirely once support ends.

insights.claim: JAM Software justifies not retaining old installers and keys indefinitely by citing security risk—older software versions can contain known bugs or vulnerabilities—plus the technical and organizational overhead of supporting legacy versions.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A reusable vendor justification pattern (security risk framing) for restricting access to legacy software versions—worth scrutinizing when evaluating similar policies elsewhere.
insights.quote: older software versions can contain known bugs or even security vulnerabilities
insights.locator: quote from Christ on security rationale

insights.claim: A Reddit user whose perpetual-license support ends in September criticized the policy as stripping paying customers of the ability to simply download an installer for software they already own.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Captures the customer-trust cost of subscription pivots for previously perpetual products.
insights.quote: Honestly, it feels quite low to strip paying customers of the ability to simply download the installer for a software version they already paid for.

insights.claim: JAM Software says it proactively emails customers before their maintenance period ends to urge them to back up their own installation file and license key, and considers this sufficient notice that no one 'loses access' to already-purchased software.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Shows the vendor's mitigation measure and how it reframes the burden of continuity onto the customer.
insights.quote: To make sure customers aren't caught off guard, we proactively notify them by email

themes.theme: Refunds refused, no clear business case for subscribing

insights.claim: JAM Software rejects refund requests from frustrated customers, arguing that since customers received indefinite use of the software version they purchased, a refund is not warranted.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Signals the vendor's legal/contractual position that 'perpetual' means perpetual use of a static version, not perpetual support—a distinction likely to recur in other vendor disputes.
insights.quote: since our customers do receive what was agreed, namely indefinite use of the software version they purchased, we don't consider a refund to be warranted

insights.claim: The article questions whether a disk-space analyzer even merits a subscription business model at all, noting free alternatives like WinDirStat exist, though TreeSize's free version lacks features such as duplicate-file search and deduplication and isn't licensed for business use.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: Raises a value-for-money question that could pressure JAM Software's subscription pricing if free competitors are seen as adequate substitutes.
insights.locator: paragraph on 'Should this be a subscription?'

insights.claim: TreeSize's shift exemplifies a broader software-industry trend of the past decade in which vendors increasingly move to subscriptions in pursuit of more predictable recurring revenue.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A macro pattern for watching other perpetual-license software vendors likely to follow the same subscription conversion playbook.

insights.claim: Christ frames the subscription model as giving JAM Software more predictability in development and support funding than one-time purchases can provide, especially amid a more challenging broader economic environment than a few years ago.
insights.speaker: Scharon Harding
insights.so_what: A vendor-side rationale linking macroeconomic pressure directly to business-model change, a pattern likely recurring across small independent software vendors.
insights.quote: A subscription model gives us that predictability in a way one-time purchases do not.
